title: US Lifts AI Model Export Curbs, SK Hynix Launches Record Chip IPO, G7 Scrambles on Minerals
subhead: Anthropic's frontier models get global release after three-week government standoff, SK Hynix launches a $28B US listing on the AI boom, and G7 critical minerals plans hit friction between US bilateralism and European multilateralism.

sections.heading: US Lifts Export Controls on Anthropic's Frontier AI Models
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department has lifted export controls on Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, ending a three-week standoff that forced the company to shut down access to anyone outside the United States. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick sent Anthropic a letter stating the company would 'no longer need a license for exports or in-country transfers' of either model, while reserving the right to re-impose curbs at any point.

Mythos 5, the more capable cybersecurity-focused model, had been flagged as 'uniquely attractive to malicious actors' — able to 'find and exploit software vulnerabilities more effectively than any other model and all but the most skilled human security experts,' according to Anthropic. The company agreed to expand its government partnership, set up a 24/7 team to monitor jailbreak threats, and create a red-teaming program with external researchers (the 'Glasswing program') for vetted cybersecurity partners.

The episode establishes a template for how the US government handles frontier AI releases: shutdown first, negotiate safety measures, then conditional redeployment. The White House then applied the same approach to OpenAI's GPT-5.6, asking the company to stagger its public release and limit initial access to a small group of vetted US-based partners — a model OpenAI complied with on June 26. France's President Macron told reporters at the G7 summit he expected progress on broadening access to Mythos 'in the coming weeks,' signaling allied pressure on the US to share frontier capabilities with trusted partners.
sections.whyItMatters: The US has effectively created an ad-hoc frontier AI export control regime — not through legislation or formal rulemaking, but through case-by-case executive pressure on individual companies. The Anthropic and OpenAI episodes suggest a pattern: deploy, restrict, negotiate, conditionally release. The question is whether this becomes durable policy or remains an unpredictable tool of presidential discretion.

sections.heading: G7 Discusses 'Trusted Partners' Access to US AI Models
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, leaders discussed a plan to grant select 'trusted partners' access to advanced AI models from US firms such as Anthropic, three diplomatic sources told Reuters. The proposal was directly prompted by the Anthropic export control episode — European allies, particularly France, pushed back hard on being locked out of frontier AI capabilities that US companies could deploy domestically.

President Macron said he expected progress on broadening access to leading US AI models within weeks, while G7 leaders pledged 'closer coordination on the risks and opportunities of frontier AI.' The discussion revealed a fundamental tension: the US wants to maintain a national security grip on the most powerful AI systems, while European allies argue that being cut off from frontier models creates an intolerable dependency gap.

The framework under discussion would create a tiered system — some models available to all G7 partners, others restricted to narrower 'trusted' groups — but no agreement was reached on the governance structure, vetting criteria, or which countries would qualify. Japan's PM Takaichi separately pushed for a coordinated G7 response to China's use of export controls and trade leverage, linking AI access to the broader tech sovereignty debate.
sections.whyItMatters: AI model access is becoming a treaty-level issue. The G7 'trusted partners' concept, if formalized, would create a new category of tech alliance — but its vagueness also means it could become another arena where the US sets terms and allies either accept or fall behind.

sections.heading: SK Hynix Launches $28B US Listing as AI Chip Demand Surges
sections.body:
South Korean memory chipmaker SK Hynix launched its approximately $28 billion US listing on July 6, one of the world's largest tech IPOs, capitalizing on the global AI investment boom. The listing comes as South Korea's exports posted their strongest growth since 1978 in June, driven by record chip shipments, according to Reuters.

SK Hynix is the dominant producer of HBM3E high-bandwidth memory chips used in Nvidia's AI accelerator GPUs. The listing on US exchanges signals that Korean chip firms see the American capital markets — and by extension the American AI infrastructure buildout — as the primary growth engine for the next decade. South Korea separately announced plans to create a 'future fund' using extra tax revenue from the semiconductor boom to invest in growth engines and address inequality.

The timing underscores the degree to which AI compute demand is reshaping global semiconductor economics: Taiwan's exports hit record highs in May 2026 powered by AI server demand, while Singapore's non-oil domestic exports jumped 38.4% year-on-year in May on AI-related shipments. China's May exports also beat forecasts, driven by chips, autos, and AI-linked tech products.
sections.whyItMatters: The AI boom is creating a narrow group of winners in the semiconductor supply chain — SK Hynix, TSMC, Samsung — concentrated in just two countries (South Korea and Taiwan). The $28B listing validates that concentration, but also deepens the geopolitical exposure: any disruption to Korean or Taiwanese chip production would hit the global AI buildout directly.

sections.heading: G7 Critical Minerals Alliance Forms, but Pricing Plan Stalls
sections.body:
G7 leaders agreed at the Evian summit to set up a Critical Minerals Alliance and cooperation platform to reduce reliance on China for rare earths and permanent magnets. The target: bring dependency on any single non-G7 supplier below 60% by 2030, with an ultimate goal of 50% 'as soon as possible.' The alliance will start with lithium and nickel as pilot minerals, expanding to five new minerals each year with a focus on rare earth elements.

However, the Trump administration's push for a Western critical minerals pricing bloc faced significant pushback. The US proposed using a DARPA-developed AI pricing model to set price floors and regulate markets — a concept that left G7 allies skeptical about governance, cost, and who pays the premium. Canada and France want a G7-led multilateral structure under the IEA or OECD, while the US prefers bilateral deals that it can negotiate quickly and expand later, three diplomatic sources told Reuters.

Japan's PM Takaichi separately proposed a joint critical minerals stockpiling initiative at the G7. The EU shortlisted tungsten, rare earths, and gallium for its first joint stockpile. The US launched its $12 billion Project Vault critical minerals reserve earlier this year. So far, G7 countries have announced 195 projects with €64 billion ($74 billion) in investment since the start of 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is finally building institutions to counter China's dominance in critical minerals — but the fundamental disagreement over whether to use multilateral frameworks or US-led bilateral deals mirrors the broader transatlantic split on tech governance. China controls 90% of processed rare earths and magnets; closing that gap requires far more than aspirational targets.

sections.heading: US Holds Off Blacklisting DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese Firms as Pentagon Adds Tech Giants to Military List
sections.body:
The Trump administration has delayed adding Chinese AI startup DeepSeek, memory chipmaker CXMT, and more than 100 other companies flagged as national security risks to the Commerce Department's Entity List, despite an interagency committee approving their inclusion last year. Two sources told Reuters the delay is intended to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing. The companies had been vetted and approved for blacklisting, but the White House never published the names.

Simultaneously, the Pentagon added Alibaba, Baidu, BYD, NIO, and Unitree to its list of Chinese companies it says are aiding the military — a different and more targeted mechanism than the Commerce Department's Entity List. Alibaba has sued the Pentagon over the designation, arguing it has no ties to China's military and that the government violated the law. Chinese biotech firm WuXi AppTec also filed suit against the US Defense Department over its military designation. China's commerce ministry said it was 'strongly dissatisfied' with the Pentagon's move.

The dual-track approach — holding off on broad export controls while sharpening military-linked designations — signals a deliberate strategy: keep commercial channels open for negotiation while building a case-by-case military restriction framework. But it also sends conflicting signals to allies and companies about the seriousness of US tech decoupling policy.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between the Pentagon's military list and Commerce's delayed Entity List reveals a policy at cross-purposes. The US is simultaneously expanding the scope of companies deemed military threats (now including consumer platforms like Alibaba and Baidu) while holding back on export controls that would actually restrict technology flows. The result is more uncertainty, not less.

sections.heading: Europe's FCAS Fighter Program Collapses, F-35 Readiness Hits 25%
sections.body:
Germany and Spain have formally launched 'Team Gen 6' to develop sixth-generation fighter technology after the collapse of the Franco-German Future Combat Air System (FCAS) program — a $100+ billion effort that produced no prototype in nine years. The breakup, driven by an irreconcilable workshare dispute between Dassault and Airbus, leaves Europe without an indigenous path to a next-generation combat aircraft. The US now has an estimated 8-10 year head start in fighter jet development, according to Antonio Fonfría, an applied economics professor at Complutense University of Madrid.

The FCAS collapse deepens Europe's dependency on the F-35 — just as a new GAO report reveals that only 25% of the F-35 fleet is fully mission capable, with readiness rates continuing to decline through fiscal 2025. Some F-35s are now being delivered without radars due to AN/APG-85 production delays. Poland has expressed interest in doubling its F-35 order to 64 units, and 13 European countries have committed to more than 600 F-35s — a figure that could reach 700.

Meanwhile, Sweden's intelligence services suspect Russia is using a Russian Orthodox church near Västerås airport for espionage and hybrid activities, and Russia is constructing facilities for tens of thousands of troops near the Arctic along NATO's northern flank, according to multiple reports.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe now faces a double bind: its own next-gen fighter program is dead, and the American替代 it must buy instead has a readiness rate of 25%. Meanwhile, Russia is building Arctic military infrastructure and running espionage operations from Swedish churches. The F-35 dependency isn't just strategic — it's operational, and the numbers don't look good.

sections.heading: Hong Kong-SenseTime AI Data Center and China's Escalating AI Price War
sections.body:
Hong Kong Science and Technology Park and SenseTime are partnering to build a domestic AI data center targeting 40,000 petaflops of computing power by 2030, amid growing competition in the Greater Bay Area. The project signals Hong Kong's push to develop indigenous AI compute capacity rather than relying entirely on mainland infrastructure. Chinese AI startup StepFun is also preparing a Hong Kong IPO, adding to the city's growing AI capital markets activity.

Across mainland China, an AI price war is deepening as narrowing capability gaps between models and falling service costs fuel competition among Chinese tech firms. Analysts predict further price cuts. The valuation gap between China's top LLM startups is widening: Zhipu is gaining while MiniMax is losing ground, reflecting market differentiation in a crowded field. Meanwhile, China's May exports surged past forecasts on the back of AI-related demand for chips and tech products, and Singapore's non-oil domestic exports jumped 38.4% year-on-year, also AI-driven.

At the same time, the AI price war is spreading globally. The Wall Street Journal reports that an 'AI price war' is piling pressure on OpenAI and Anthropic as enterprise customers turn to cheaper models — including some from China — reducing margins for frontier model providers just as they prepare major IPOs.
sections.whyItMatters: China is rapidly building domestic AI compute capacity and capital markets infrastructure in Hong Kong, while simultaneously commoditizing AI model prices. The combination of falling model costs and rising compute investment means Chinese AI firms are betting on volume and hardware, not frontier model dominance — a different strategy than the US approach, and one that could flood the market with capable, cheap AI services.

bottomLine: This week crystallized a new phase in the AI sovereignty contest. The US has moved from ad-hoc model restrictions (Anthropic, OpenAI) to discussing a formal G7 'trusted partners' framework for frontier AI access — but the governance details remain undefined, and France is already pushing back. Meanwhile, SK Hynix's $28B listing validates the AI-driven chip concentration in South Korea and Taiwan, the G7 set up a critical minerals alliance that exposed a US-Europe split over bilateral vs. multilateral approaches, and Europe's FCAS fighter collapse deepened F-35 dependency just as GAO data shows only 1 in 4 F-35s is fully mission capable. China, for its part, is simultaneously building AI data centers in Hong Kong, fighting an AI price war that's commoditizing frontier models, and seeing its exports surge on AI demand — even as the Pentagon adds its biggest consumer platforms to a military list while the White House holds off on the export controls that would actually restrict technology flows.
sources: Reuters, Ars Technica, CNBC, WSJ, The Hindu Business Line, US News & World Report, OilPrice, Defense News, GAO, El Pais, Chosun, AP, TechCrunch, Forbes, Simple Flying, MSN, IISS, Singapore Ministry of Trade, South Korean Ministry of Trade, Reuters (SK Hynix), Channel News Asia, Fast Company, The Atlantic, The Guardian, Yahoo Finance, Straits Times, Nikkei Asia
